questions.question: Which of the following BEST defines accounting?

options.A: The process of recording only cash transactions of a business
options.B: The art of recording, classifying, summarising, and interpreting financial transactions
options.C: The preparation of tax returns for a business
options.D: The management of a company's investments

questions.explanation: Accounting involves recording, classifying, summarising, and communicating financial information to help users make economic decisions.

questions.question: The going concern concept assumes that

options.A: A business will be liquidated in the near future
options.B: A business will continue to operate indefinitely
options.C: All assets must be recorded at their current market value
options.D: Profits should be recognised only when cash is received

questions.explanation: The going concern concept assumes that a business will continue to operate for the foreseeable future, so assets are valued at cost rather than liquidation value.

questions.question: The accruals (matching) concept requires that

options.A: Revenue and expenses are recorded only when cash changes hands
options.B: Revenue and expenses are matched to the period in which they are earned or incurred
options.C: All expenses must be paid before year end
options.D: Revenue must exceed expenses in every accounting period

questions.explanation: The accruals concept states that income and expenditure should be recognised in the period they are earned or incurred, not when cash is received or paid.

questions.question: The prudence (conservatism) concept states that

options.A: Profits should be recognised as early as possible
options.B: Anticipated profits should not be recorded but anticipated losses should be provided for
options.C: Assets should always be recorded at their highest possible value
options.D: All expenses, whether certain or not, should be charged immediately

questions.explanation: Prudence requires accountants not to overstate profits or assets — anticipated losses are recognised immediately but anticipated gains are only recorded when certain.

questions.question: The consistency concept in accounting means that

options.A: A business must use the same accounting methods from one period to the next
options.B: All accountants in a company must use the same methods
options.C: Financial statements must be prepared on the same day each year
options.D: A business must use internationally recognised accounting standards

questions.explanation: The consistency concept requires that once a business adopts an accounting method (e.g., depreciation method), it continues to use the same method in subsequent periods, enabling meaningful comparisons.

questions.question: The business entity concept requires that

options.A: All businesses in the same industry use the same accounting methods
options.B: The business is treated as separate from its owners for accounting purposes
options.C: Only large businesses need to prepare financial statements
options.D: A business must register with the government before recording transactions

questions.explanation: The business entity concept treats the business as a separate entity from its owners. The owner's personal transactions are kept separate from those of the business.

questions.question: The money measurement concept states that

options.A: Only transactions denominated in foreign currency are recorded
options.B: Only transactions that can be expressed in monetary terms are recorded in the accounts
options.C: All assets must be valued at their current money value
options.D: Money paid for assets is more important than their replacement cost

questions.explanation: The money measurement concept means accounting only records transactions that can be expressed in monetary terms. Non-monetary items like staff morale or reputation are not recorded.

questions.question: Capital expenditure is expenditure on

options.A: Day-to-day running costs of a business such as rent and wages
options.B: Acquiring fixed assets or improving their earning capacity
options.C: Purchasing goods for resale
options.D: Paying dividends to shareholders

questions.explanation: Capital expenditure is spending on fixed assets (e.g., buying machinery, vehicles) or improving existing assets. It appears on the balance sheet, not the profit and loss account.

questions.question: Revenue expenditure is expenditure on

options.A: The purchase of fixed assets for use in the business
options.B: Day-to-day running expenses of the business
options.C: Extending or improving a fixed asset
options.D: Repaying long-term loans

questions.explanation: Revenue expenditure covers the day-to-day operating costs of a business (rent, wages, electricity, repairs). It is charged to the profit and loss account in the period incurred.

questions.question: The dual aspect (duality) concept states that

options.A: A business must have two bank accounts
options.B: Every transaction has two equal and opposite effects — a debit and a credit
options.C: All accounts must be balanced twice a year
options.D: Financial statements must be prepared in two currencies

questions.explanation: The dual aspect (double-entry) concept means every transaction affects at least two accounts — for every debit there is an equal credit, maintaining the accounting equation: Assets = Liabilities + Capital.

questions.question: A source document is

options.A: The final account prepared at year end
options.B: The original written evidence for a business transaction
options.C: A summary of all ledger accounts
options.D: The auditor's report on the financial statements

questions.explanation: A source document is the original evidence of a business transaction — examples include invoices, receipts, credit notes, cheque stubs, and bank statements.

questions.question: Which source document is used to record a credit sale?

options.A: Debit note
options.B: Receipt
options.C: Sales invoice
options.D: Credit note

questions.explanation: A sales invoice is issued by the seller to the buyer for goods sold on credit. It is the source document used to record a credit sale in the sales day book.

questions.question: A credit note is used to record

options.A: A cash payment made by the business
options.B: Goods returned by a customer or an overcharge to a customer
options.C: Interest charged on an overdue account
options.D: A new credit sale made to a customer

questions.explanation: A credit note is issued by the seller when goods are returned by the buyer or when the buyer was overcharged. It reduces the amount the buyer owes.

questions.question: The sales day book (journal) is used to record

options.A: All sales, both cash and credit
options.B: Only credit sales of goods
options.C: Cash received from customers
options.D: Returns of goods by customers

questions.explanation: The sales day book records all credit sales of goods only. Cash sales are recorded directly in the cash book, not the sales day book.

questions.question: The purchases day book is used to record

options.A: All purchases, both cash and credit
options.B: Only credit purchases of goods for resale
options.C: Cash paid to suppliers
options.D: Goods returned to suppliers

questions.explanation: The purchases day book records only credit purchases of goods for resale. Cash purchases go directly to the cash book.

questions.question: The returns inwards (sales returns) day book records

options.A: Goods purchased and returned to the supplier
options.B: Goods returned by customers to the business
options.C: Cash refunds given to customers
options.D: Damaged goods written off by the business

questions.explanation: Returns inwards (sales returns) are goods returned by customers. They are recorded in the returns inwards day book and are posted as a debit to sales returns account and a credit to the customer's account.

questions.question: The journal proper (general journal) is used to record

options.A: All daily cash transactions
options.B: Transactions that do not fit in any other book of original entry — such as correction of errors and opening entries
options.C: Monthly summaries of all ledger accounts
options.D: Bank transactions only

questions.explanation: The journal proper (general journal) records unusual or non-routine transactions including: opening entries, correction of errors, purchase/sale of fixed assets on credit, depreciation, and bad debts written off.

questions.question: The cash book serves as both a book of original entry and a

options.A: Trial balance
options.B: Ledger account (the cash account and bank account in the ledger)
options.C: Final account
options.D: Source document

questions.explanation: The cash book is a book of prime entry for all cash and bank transactions. It also serves as the cash account and bank account in the ledger — making it both a book of original entry and a ledger account.

questions.question: A petty cash book is maintained to record

options.A: Large payments made by cheque
options.B: Small cash payments for minor expenses
options.C: All transactions involving foreign currency
options.D: Monthly salaries paid to employees

questions.explanation: A petty cash book records small, routine cash payments (e.g., postage, stationery, bus fares). It operates on the imprest system — a fixed float is topped up to the same amount at the start of each period.

questions.question: Under the imprest system of petty cash, the petty cashier is reimbursed

options.A: A fixed amount at the beginning of every month
options.B: The exact amount spent during the period to restore the float to its original level
options.C: All cash collected from customers
options.D: The total of all receipts during the period

questions.explanation: Under the imprest system, the petty cashier is given a fixed float. At the end of the period, the main cashier reimburses exactly the amount spent, restoring the float to its original level.

questions.question: In double-entry bookkeeping, which of the following is correct?

options.A: All debits are on the right side of an account
options.B: For every debit entry there is a corresponding credit entry of equal value
options.C: Only liabilities are credited
options.D: Cash accounts are always debited

questions.explanation: Double-entry bookkeeping requires that every transaction is recorded as a debit in one account and an equal credit in another account, keeping the accounting equation in balance.

questions.question: When a business purchases goods for cash, the correct double entry is

options.A: Debit purchases account; Credit cash account
options.B: Debit cash account; Credit purchases account
options.C: Debit sales account; Credit cash account
options.D: Debit cash account; Credit sales account

questions.explanation: Purchasing goods increases the purchases account (debit) and decreases cash (credit). Rule: Debit what comes in; Credit what goes out — but for nominal accounts, debit all expenses.

questions.question: When a business sells goods on credit to a customer, the double entry is

options.A: Debit sales account; Credit debtor's account
options.B: Debit debtor's account; Credit sales account
options.C: Debit cash account; Credit sales account
options.D: Debit sales account; Credit cash account

questions.explanation: A credit sale creates a debtor (asset increases — debit) and recognises revenue (sales account — credit). Debit the debtor's account; Credit the sales account.

questions.question: The accounting equation is expressed as

options.A: Assets = Revenue – Expenses
options.B: Assets = Capital + Liabilities
options.C: Assets = Liabilities – Capital
options.D: Capital = Assets + Liabilities

questions.explanation: The fundamental accounting equation is: Assets = Capital + Liabilities. Every transaction maintains this equality through the double-entry system.

questions.question: A debit balance on a ledger account indicates

options.A: The account has a credit balance brought forward
options.B: The account has more debits than credits — typically an asset or expense
options.C: The account belongs to a creditor
options.D: The business owes money to the account holder

questions.explanation: A debit balance (total debits > total credits) typically indicates an asset, expense, or drawings account. A credit balance typically indicates a liability, income, or capital account.

questions.question: When rent is paid by cheque, the double entry is

options.A: Debit bank account; Credit rent account
options.B: Debit rent account; Credit bank account
options.C: Debit rent account; Credit cash account
options.D: Debit capital account; Credit rent account

questions.explanation: Paying rent is an expense (debit rent account) and reduces the bank balance (credit bank account).

questions.question: Drawing cash from the bank for office use requires

options.A: Debit bank account; Credit cash account
options.B: Debit cash account; Credit bank account
options.C: Debit capital account; Credit bank account
options.D: Debit drawings account; Credit bank account

questions.explanation: Withdrawing cash from the bank for office use: cash increases (debit cash account) and bank decreases (credit bank account). This is a contra entry in the cash book.

questions.question: When a debtor pays by cheque, the double entry is

options.A: Debit debtor's account; Credit bank account
options.B: Debit bank account; Credit debtor's account
options.C: Debit cash account; Credit debtor's account
options.D: Debit sales account; Credit bank account

questions.explanation: When a debtor pays by cheque: bank increases (debit bank account) and the debtor's balance is reduced (credit debtor's account).

questions.question: A trial balance is prepared to

options.A: Show the financial position of a business
options.B: Verify that total debits equal total credits in the ledger
options.C: Calculate the profit for the year
options.D: Show cash balances only

questions.explanation: A trial balance is extracted from the ledger to check the arithmetic accuracy of the double-entry system — if total debits equal total credits, the ledger is in balance (though some errors may still exist).

questions.question: Which of the following errors will NOT be revealed by a trial balance?

options.A: A transaction posted to the wrong side of an account
options.B: An error of omission — a transaction completely omitted from the books
options.C: An arithmetic error in totalling a ledger account
options.D: A transposition error in posting an amount

questions.explanation: A trial balance will NOT detect errors of omission (both debit and credit omitted), errors of commission (wrong account but correct side), errors of principle, and compensating errors, because they leave the trial balance still balanced.

questions.question: Which of the following items appears on the debit side of a trial balance?

options.A: Capital
options.B: Trade creditors
options.C: Carriage inwards
options.D: Bank overdraft

questions.explanation: Debit balances include assets, expenses, and drawings. Carriage inwards is an expense (debit). Capital, creditors, and bank overdraft are credit balances.

questions.question: A provision for bad debts account has a credit balance because

options.A: It represents cash received from debtors
options.B: It is a reduction of the asset (debtors) on the balance sheet
options.C: It is an income item in the profit and loss account
options.D: It represents money owed by the business

questions.explanation: A provision for bad debts is a credit balance — it is a contra asset account that reduces the value of debtors on the balance sheet to reflect the amount expected to be collected.

questions.question: A bank reconciliation statement is prepared to

options.A: Show the profit made from bank transactions
options.B: Reconcile the difference between the cash book bank balance and the bank statement balance
options.C: Confirm the amount owed to the bank
options.D: Show all transactions processed by the bank in the year

questions.explanation: A bank reconciliation statement explains the differences between the balance in the firm's cash book and the balance shown on the bank statement, due to timing differences and bank errors.

questions.question: Unpresented (outstanding) cheques are cheques that

options.A: Have been returned by the bank due to insufficient funds
options.B: Have been written and recorded in the cash book but not yet presented to the bank for payment
options.C: Have been cancelled by the drawer before being cashed
options.D: Were issued in the previous accounting period

questions.explanation: Unpresented cheques have been issued and recorded in the cash book as a credit, but have not yet been presented to the bank. They cause the cash book balance to be lower than the bank statement balance.

questions.question: Outstanding lodgements (uncleared deposits) are amounts that

options.A: Have been credited in the cash book but not yet credited by the bank on the statement
options.B: Have been debited by the bank but not yet recorded in the cash book
options.C: Are disputed transactions between the firm and the bank
options.D: Represent dishonoured cheques returned by the bank

questions.explanation: Outstanding lodgements (deposits in transit) are cash/cheques recorded as debits in the firm's cash book but not yet shown as credits on the bank statement because the bank has not processed them yet.

questions.question: Bank charges appear on the bank statement but not in the cash book. When updating the cash book, bank charges should be

options.A: Added to the cash book balance
options.B: Deducted from the cash book balance (credited in the cash book)
options.C: Ignored as they are a bank error
options.D: Added to the bank statement balance

questions.explanation: Bank charges are debited by the bank on the bank statement. To update the cash book, they are recorded as a credit in the cash book (reducing the cash book balance).

questions.question: A sales ledger control account (debtors control account) is used to

options.A: Record all credit purchases from suppliers
options.B: Provide a summary total of all individual debtors' balances as a check on the sales ledger
options.C: Record cash sales made during the period
options.D: Control the amount of credit given to each customer

questions.explanation: The sales ledger control account contains summary totals of all transactions affecting debtors. Its closing balance should equal the sum of all individual debtors' account balances in the sales ledger.

questions.question: Which of the following will be debited in the purchases ledger control account?

options.A: Credit purchases during the period
options.B: Returns outwards (goods returned to suppliers)
options.C: Discount received from suppliers
options.D: Payments made to suppliers

questions.explanation: The purchases ledger control account is credited with credit purchases (increasing what we owe) and debited with payments to suppliers, returns outwards, and discounts received (reducing what we owe).

questions.question: An error of commission occurs when

options.A: A transaction is completely omitted from the books
options.B: A transaction is posted to the correct side but to the wrong personal account
options.C: A transaction is recorded using the wrong accounting principle
options.D: Two errors cancel each other out

questions.explanation: An error of commission is when the correct amount is posted to the correct side (debit/credit) but to the wrong account — e.g., posting a sale to the account of the wrong customer.

questions.question: An error of principle occurs when

options.A: A transaction is posted to the wrong personal account
options.B: A transaction is entered in the wrong class of account — e.g., treating capital expenditure as revenue expenditure
options.C: Both debit and credit entries for a transaction are omitted
options.D: The wrong amount is entered on both sides of a transaction

questions.explanation: An error of principle is when a transaction is recorded in the wrong type of account — e.g., debiting repairs (revenue) instead of machinery (capital). The trial balance still balances.

questions.question: A compensating error occurs when

options.A: An error on one account is cancelled by an equal error on another account
options.B: A transaction is recorded twice in the books
options.C: The correct amount is posted to the wrong account
options.D: A transaction is posted to the wrong side of the correct account

questions.explanation: Compensating errors are two or more errors that cancel each other out — one error is equal and opposite to another, so the trial balance still agrees despite the errors.

questions.question: A suspense account is opened when

options.A: A new business is started
options.B: The trial balance does not balance — to hold the difference temporarily until errors are found
options.C: The business wants to defer payment of a liability
options.D: An accountant is unsure whether to record a transaction

questions.explanation: When the trial balance does not agree, the difference is placed in a suspense account. As errors are found and corrected, journal entries are made to clear the suspense account until its balance becomes zero.

questions.question: The reversal of entries error occurs when

options.A: A transaction is completely omitted from the books
options.B: The correct amounts are entered but the debit and credit are reversed
options.C: Two transactions are merged into one entry
options.D: The same transaction is recorded in two different books

questions.explanation: A reversal of entries error means the correct accounts are used and the correct amounts recorded, but the debit and credit are swapped — e.g., debiting the creditor instead of crediting, and crediting purchases instead of debiting.

questions.question: Depreciation is defined as

options.A: The physical wear and tear of a fixed asset only
options.B: The systematic allocation of the cost of a fixed asset over its useful economic life
options.C: The reduction in market value of an asset due to inflation
options.D: The cost of repairing a fixed asset

questions.explanation: Depreciation is the process of spreading the cost of a fixed asset over its useful life. It matches the cost of the asset against the revenues it helps generate.

questions.question: Under the straight-line method of depreciation, the annual depreciation charge is

options.A: Higher in the early years and reduces over time
options.B: A constant amount each year throughout the asset's useful life
options.C: Based on the asset's net book value at the start of each year
options.D: Variable depending on the usage of the asset each year

questions.explanation: The straight-line method charges an equal (fixed) amount of depreciation each year: Annual depreciation = (Cost – Residual value) ÷ Useful life.

questions.question: The reducing balance (diminishing balance) method of depreciation charges

options.A: The same amount of depreciation every year
options.B: A fixed percentage of the asset's net book value each year — higher in early years and decreasing over time
options.C: Depreciation only in the years the asset is used
options.D: Depreciation based on the units produced by the asset

questions.explanation: The reducing balance method applies a fixed percentage to the asset's net book value (cost less accumulated depreciation). This gives higher depreciation in early years, which reduces over time.

questions.question: A machine costs ₦50,000, has a residual value of ₦5,000, and a useful life of 5 years. Annual depreciation using the straight-line method is
questions.explanation: Annual depreciation = (Cost – Residual value) ÷ Useful life = (₦50,000 – ₦5,000) ÷ 5 = ₦45,000 ÷ 5 = ₦9,000 per year.

questions.question: The net book value (NBV) of an asset is

options.A: The current market value of the asset
options.B: The cost of the asset less accumulated depreciation to date
options.C: The insurance value of the asset
options.D: The residual value of the asset at the end of its life

questions.explanation: Net Book Value (NBV) = Cost of asset – Accumulated depreciation. It represents the carrying amount of the asset in the books.

questions.question: When an asset is disposed of at a profit (more than its book value), the double entry is

options.A: Debit asset account; Credit disposal account
options.B: Debit disposal account; Credit profit on disposal in P&L account
options.C: Debit profit on disposal; Credit disposal account
options.D: Debit disposal account; Credit bank; and debit bank; Credit profit on disposal

questions.explanation: When the proceeds exceed the net book value, a profit on disposal arises. The profit is credited to the profit and loss account. The disposal account collects all entries relating to the asset's disposal.

questions.question: A bad debt is

options.A: A debt that is very large in amount
options.B: A debt that is considered irrecoverable and is written off
options.C: A debt that is overdue by more than 30 days
options.D: A debt owed to the business by a foreign customer

questions.explanation: A bad debt is a debt from a debtor that the business is certain will never be collected. It is written off by debiting the bad debts account and crediting the debtor's account.

questions.question: When a bad debt is written off, the double entry is

options.A: Debit debtor's account; Credit bad debts account
options.B: Debit bad debts account; Credit debtor's account
options.C: Debit bad debts account; Credit provision for bad debts account
options.D: Debit sales account; Credit debtor's account

questions.explanation: Writing off a bad debt: Debit bad debts account (expense increases) and Credit debtor's account (asset decreases — the amount owed is removed).

questions.question: A provision for doubtful debts is created to

options.A: Write off all bad debts immediately
options.B: Estimate and provide for debts that may not be collected in the future
options.C: Record cash received from previously bad debts
options.D: Increase the profit of the business

questions.explanation: A provision for doubtful debts is an estimate of debts that may not be collected. It is created by debiting the profit and loss account and crediting the provision for doubtful debts account.

questions.question: The gross profit is calculated as

options.A: Net sales minus all expenses
options.B: Net sales minus cost of goods sold
options.C: Total revenue minus total expenses
options.D: Sales minus purchases

questions.explanation: Gross profit = Net sales – Cost of Goods Sold (COGS). COGS = Opening stock + Purchases + Carriage inwards – Returns outwards – Closing stock.

questions.question: Carriage inwards is

options.A: The cost of delivering goods to customers
options.B: The transport cost of bringing goods purchased into the business — included in cost of goods sold
options.C: Income earned from delivering goods to customers
options.D: The cost of transporting fixed assets to the business

questions.explanation: Carriage inwards is the cost of transporting purchased goods to the business. It is included in the trading account (cost of goods sold) as it is a direct cost of obtaining the goods.

questions.question: Carriage outwards is

options.A: A direct cost included in the trading account
options.B: The cost of delivering goods to customers — charged as an expense in the profit and loss account
options.C: The transport cost of returning goods to suppliers
options.D: Income received from customers for delivery services

questions.explanation: Carriage outwards is the cost of delivering goods to customers. It is an indirect selling expense charged in the profit and loss account (not the trading account).

questions.question: Net profit is calculated as

options.A: Gross profit minus returns inwards
options.B: Gross profit minus all other operating expenses (indirect expenses)
options.C: Total income minus cost of goods sold
options.D: Sales minus all liabilities

questions.explanation: Net profit = Gross profit – Indirect (operating) expenses. Indirect expenses include: rent, wages, salaries, insurance, depreciation, bad debts, advertising, carriage outwards, etc.

questions.question: Opening stock ₦10,000; Purchases ₦40,000; Returns outwards ₦2,000; Closing stock ₦8,000. Cost of goods sold is
Sales ₦100,000; Cost of goods sold ₦60,000; Operating expenses ₦25,000. Net profit is
A balance sheet shows

options.A: The profit or loss made during an accounting period
options.B: The financial position of a business at a specific date — assets, liabilities, and capital
options.C: The cash flows of a business during a period
options.D: The revenues and expenses of a business for a year

questions.explanation: A balance sheet (statement of financial position) is a snapshot of a business's financial position at a specific date, showing assets, liabilities, and owner's capital.

questions.question: Current liabilities are obligations that

options.A: Are due for payment in more than one year
options.B: Are due for payment within one year or the normal operating cycle
options.C: Are secured against fixed assets
options.D: Are owed to the owners of the business

questions.explanation: Current liabilities are short-term debts payable within one year — examples include trade creditors, bank overdraft, accruals, and short-term loans.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-current (fixed) asset?

options.A: Stock/inventory
options.B: Trade debtors
options.C: Land and buildings
options.D: Prepaid expenses

questions.explanation: Non-current (fixed) assets are long-term assets used in the business — land, buildings, machinery, vehicles, furniture. Stock, debtors, and prepayments are current assets.

questions.question: The owner's equity (capital) section of a balance sheet includes

options.A: Long-term loans and debentures
options.B: Opening capital plus net profit minus drawings
options.C: Current liabilities and accruals
options.D: Fixed assets and current assets

questions.explanation: Owner's equity = Opening capital + Net profit (or – Net loss) – Drawings. It represents the owner's claim on the net assets of the business.

questions.question: Accrued expenses (accruals) on the balance sheet represent

options.A: Expenses paid in advance for a future period
options.B: Expenses incurred but not yet paid at the balance sheet date
options.C: Income earned but not yet received
options.D: Cash held in the business at year end

questions.explanation: Accruals are expenses that have been incurred in the period but not yet paid — e.g., accrued wages, accrued rent. They appear as current liabilities on the balance sheet.

questions.question: Prepaid expenses on the balance sheet represent

options.A: Expenses incurred but not yet paid
options.B: Expenses paid in advance that relate to a future accounting period
options.C: Income received in advance from customers
options.D: Goods purchased but not yet delivered

questions.explanation: Prepayments are payments made for expenses that relate to a future period — e.g., insurance paid in advance. They are treated as current assets on the balance sheet.

questions.question: Deferred income (income received in advance) on the balance sheet is treated as

options.A: Current asset
options.B: Non-current asset
options.C: Current liability
options.D: Long-term liability

questions.explanation: Income received in advance (deferred income) is a current liability because the business has received payment but has not yet provided the goods or services — an obligation remains.

questions.question: A business pays rent of ₦24,000 for the year, but only ₦18,000 relates to the current year. The prepayment is
questions.explanation: Prepayment = Amount paid – Amount relating to current year = ₦24,000 – ₦18,000 = ₦6,000. This ₦6,000 is a current asset on the balance sheet.

questions.question: If electricity for a year is ₦12,000 but only ₦9,000 has been paid by year end, the charge to the profit and loss account is
questions.explanation: The full ₦12,000 is charged to the P&L account for the year (accruals concept). The unpaid ₦3,000 is an accrual (current liability) on the balance sheet.

questions.question: The statement of affairs is used in incomplete records to

options.A: Calculate the gross profit of the business
options.B: Determine the opening or closing capital when proper records are not kept
options.C: Show all transactions for the year
options.D: Reconcile cash book differences

questions.explanation: A statement of affairs is a simplified balance sheet used in incomplete records to calculate capital: Capital = Assets – Liabilities. It is used when a proper balance sheet is not available.

questions.question: In incomplete records, net profit is calculated as

options.A: Total sales minus total purchases
options.B: Closing capital – Opening capital + Drawings – Additional capital introduced
options.C: Total receipts minus total payments
options.D: Gross profit minus operating expenses

questions.explanation: In incomplete records: Net profit = Closing capital – Opening capital + Drawings – Additional capital introduced. This is the capital comparison method.

questions.question: Mark-up in trading refers to

options.A: Profit as a percentage of selling price
options.B: Profit as a percentage of cost price
options.C: The difference between list price and selling price
options.D: The percentage increase in sales from one year to the next

questions.explanation: Mark-up = (Gross profit ÷ Cost of goods sold) × 100. It expresses profit as a percentage of the cost price.

questions.question: Margin (gross profit margin) is

options.A: Profit as a percentage of cost of goods sold
options.B: Profit as a percentage of sales (selling price)
options.C: The absolute value of gross profit
options.D: The ratio of net profit to gross profit

questions.explanation: Gross profit margin = (Gross profit ÷ Sales) × 100. It expresses profit as a percentage of the selling price.

questions.question: A partnership appropriation account is used to

options.A: Record the profits and losses from trading activities
options.B: Show how net profit is divided among partners — salaries, interest on capital, and profit shares
options.C: Record drawings made by each partner
options.D: Calculate the total capital invested by all partners

questions.explanation: The partnership appropriation account (profit distribution account) shows how the net profit is allocated among partners: first deducting partners' salaries and interest on capital, then sharing the remainder in the agreed profit-sharing ratio.

questions.question: Interest on capital in a partnership is

options.A: A charge against the business to reward partners for their capital contributions
options.B: Interest paid to the bank on partner loans
options.C: A penalty charged when partners withdraw capital
options.D: Tax paid on the partnership's profits

questions.explanation: Interest on capital is an appropriation of profit (not an expense) that rewards partners for the capital they have contributed to the partnership.

questions.question: A partner's current account records

options.A: The permanent capital contributed by the partner
options.B: Transactions between the partner and the partnership — salaries, interest, drawings, and profit shares
options.C: Only the drawings made by the partner
options.D: The share of losses borne by the partner

questions.explanation: A partner's current account records appropriations (salaries, interest on capital, profit shares) as credits and drawings and interest on drawings as debits. It shows the day-to-day fluctuating balance owed to or by each partner.

questions.question: Goodwill in a partnership is recorded when

options.A: The business makes extra profit in a year
options.B: A new partner joins or an existing partner leaves — to compensate existing partners for the value built up
options.C: The business purchases a new fixed asset
options.D: The partnership takes out an insurance policy

questions.explanation: Goodwill represents the value of the partnership's reputation and customer base. It is recognised when there is a change in profit-sharing ratios, a new partner is admitted, or a partner retires/dies.

questions.question: Partners' fixed capital accounts record

options.A: All transactions between partners and the firm including drawings
options.B: Only the original permanent capital contributed and any additional capital formally introduced
options.C: Interest on capital only
options.D: Profit and loss shares of each partner

questions.explanation: Fixed capital accounts record only the permanent capital contributed by each partner. All other transactions (drawings, interest, profit shares) are recorded in the current account.

questions.question: In the absence of a partnership agreement, the Partnership Act provides that

options.A: Profits are shared in proportion to capital contributions
options.B: No partner receives a salary and profits are shared equally
options.C: Interest at 5% is charged on all drawings
options.D: The senior partner receives a higher share of profits

questions.explanation: Under the Partnership Act (in the absence of a deed), profits and losses are shared equally, no partner receives a salary, and no interest is paid on capital or charged on drawings.

questions.question: Share premium account arises when

options.A: Shares are issued at below their nominal (par) value
options.B: Shares are issued at above their nominal (par) value
options.C: A company earns unusually high profits
options.D: A company revalues its fixed assets upwards

questions.explanation: Share premium is the amount received above the nominal (par/face) value of shares when they are issued. E.g., if a ₦1 share is issued for ₦1.50, the ₦0.50 excess goes to the share premium account.

questions.question: A bonus (scrip/capitalisation) issue of shares means

options.A: New shares are offered to existing shareholders at a discount
options.B: Free shares are issued to existing shareholders from reserves, with no cash received by the company
options.C: New shares are offered to the general public
options.D: Directors receive extra shares as a bonus

questions.explanation: A bonus issue (capitalisation issue) converts reserves (e.g., share premium, retained earnings) into issued share capital by distributing free shares to existing shareholders in proportion to their holdings. No cash is received.

questions.question: Retained earnings (reserves) in a company's balance sheet represent

options.A: Cash held in the company's bank account
options.B: Accumulated profits that have not been distributed as dividends
options.C: Capital raised from the public through share issues
options.D: Loans made by shareholders to the company

questions.explanation: Retained earnings are the cumulative net profits of the company that have been retained (not paid out as dividends). They form part of shareholders' equity on the balance sheet.

questions.question: Debenture interest is charged

options.A: To the appropriation account after tax
options.B: As an expense in the profit and loss account before calculating net profit
options.C: As a deduction from shareholders' equity
options.D: Only when the company makes a profit

questions.explanation: Debenture interest is a fixed interest charge on a loan (debt), not a distribution of profit. It is an expense charged in the profit and loss account before arriving at net profit, unlike dividends.

questions.question: The receipts and payments account of a club shows

options.A: Only the income and expenditure of the club for the year
options.B: All actual cash receipts and payments during the period — regardless of whether they relate to the current period
options.C: The financial position of the club at a given date
options.D: The profit or loss of the club's trading activities

questions.explanation: The receipts and payments account is a simple summary of all actual cash received and paid during the period. It includes capital items (asset purchases) and does not apply the accruals concept.

questions.question: The income and expenditure account of a non-profit organisation is equivalent to the

options.A: Balance sheet of a trading business
options.B: Profit and loss account of a trading business
options.C: Trading account of a trading business
options.D: Cash flow statement of a trading business

questions.explanation: The income and expenditure account of a club/society is the equivalent of the profit and loss account of a trading business. Instead of profit or loss, it shows a surplus or deficit for the period.

questions.question: The accumulated fund of a non-profit organisation is the equivalent of

options.A: The share capital of a company
options.B: The owner's capital in a sole trader business
options.C: The debenture account in a company
options.D: The profit and loss reserve of a company

questions.explanation: The accumulated fund (general fund) of a club is equivalent to the owner's capital in a business. It represents the difference between total assets and total liabilities of the club.

questions.question: A club's bar trading account shows

options.A: The club's overall surplus or deficit for the year
options.B: The profit or loss from the club's bar operations specifically
options.C: All cash received and paid by the bar
options.D: Members' subscriptions received in the year

questions.explanation: Clubs often have separate trading activities (bar, restaurant, shop). A bar trading account is prepared to determine the profit or loss from bar operations, which is then transferred to the income and expenditure account.

questions.question: A manufacturing account is prepared to determine

options.A: The profit from selling manufactured goods
options.B: The total cost of goods manufactured during the period
options.C: The value of closing stock of finished goods
options.D: The cost of raw materials purchased

questions.explanation: A manufacturing account calculates the total (factory) cost of production, including raw materials consumed, direct labour, and factory overhead — known as the cost of production or cost of goods manufactured.

questions.question: Prime cost in a manufacturing account consists of

options.A: All production costs including factory overheads
options.B: Direct materials + Direct labour + Direct expenses
options.C: Factory overhead costs only
options.D: Selling and distribution costs

questions.explanation: Prime cost is the total of all direct costs: Direct materials consumed + Direct labour (wages) + Direct expenses (e.g., royalties). It is the first subtotal in a manufacturing account.

questions.question: Factory overhead costs are

options.A: Direct costs that can be traced to individual units of production
options.B: Indirect manufacturing costs such as factory rent, factory depreciation, and supervisors' wages
options.C: Selling and administrative expenses
options.D: The cost of raw materials wasted in production

questions.explanation: Factory overheads (works overhead, factory indirect costs) include: factory rent, rates, insurance, factory depreciation, indirect labour (supervisors), factory power, and maintenance.

questions.question: Work-in-progress (WIP) refers to

options.A: Raw materials not yet used in production
options.B: Partially completed goods that are still in the production process at year end
options.C: Finished goods awaiting sale
options.D: Goods returned by customers that need repair

questions.explanation: Work-in-progress (WIP) represents partially completed units at various stages of manufacture at the year-end date. It is valued at the cost incurred to date.

questions.question: The current ratio is calculated as

options.A: Current assets ÷ Current liabilities
options.B: Current liabilities ÷ Current assets
options.C: Fixed assets ÷ Current liabilities
options.D: Total assets ÷ Total liabilities

questions.explanation: Current ratio = Current assets ÷ Current liabilities. It measures a business's ability to pay its short-term debts with its short-term assets. A ratio of 2:1 is considered healthy.

questions.question: The acid test (quick) ratio is calculated as

options.A: (Current assets – Stock) ÷ Current liabilities
options.B: Current assets ÷ Current liabilities
options.C: (Current assets – Debtors) ÷ Current liabilities
options.D: Net profit ÷ Sales × 100

questions.explanation: Acid test ratio = (Current assets – Stock) ÷ Current liabilities. Stock is excluded because it is the least liquid current asset. A ratio of at least 1:1 is generally considered safe.

questions.question: The gross profit margin is calculated as

options.A: (Net profit ÷ Sales) × 100
options.B: (Gross profit ÷ Sales) × 100
options.C: (Gross profit ÷ Cost of sales) × 100
options.D: (Net profit ÷ Capital) × 100

questions.explanation: Gross profit margin = (Gross profit ÷ Sales) × 100. It shows what percentage of sales revenue remains after deducting the cost of goods sold.

questions.question: Return on capital employed (ROCE) measures

options.A: How efficiently a business collects its debts
options.B: The profit generated as a percentage of the capital employed in the business
options.C: The proportion of assets financed by long-term debt
options.D: How many times stock is turned over in a year

questions.explanation: ROCE = (Net profit ÷ Capital employed) × 100. It measures the efficiency and profitability of capital employed in the business — the higher, the better.

questions.question: Stock turnover ratio is calculated as

options.A: Sales ÷ Closing stock
options.B: Cost of goods sold ÷ Average stock
options.C: Gross profit ÷ Stock
options.D: Closing stock ÷ Cost of goods sold × 365

questions.explanation: Stock turnover = Cost of goods sold ÷ Average stock. It shows how many times stock is sold and replaced during a year. A higher ratio indicates faster-moving stock.

questions.question: Debtors collection period is calculated as

options.A: (Debtors ÷ Sales) × 365
options.B: (Creditors ÷ Purchases) × 365
options.C: (Stock ÷ Cost of sales) × 365
options.D: (Current assets ÷ Current liabilities) × 365

questions.explanation: Debtors collection period = (Trade debtors ÷ Credit sales) × 365 days. It shows the average number of days it takes to collect payment from debtors. A lower figure is preferable.

questions.question: If current assets are ₦80,000 and current liabilities are ₦40,000, the current ratio is
questions.explanation: Current ratio = Current assets ÷ Current liabilities = ₦80,000 ÷ ₦40,000 = 2:1. This means for every ₦1 of current liabilities, there are ₦2 of current assets.

questions.question: The net profit margin is calculated as

options.A: (Gross profit ÷ Sales) × 100
options.B: (Net profit ÷ Sales) × 100
options.C: (Net profit ÷ Cost of goods sold) × 100
options.D: (Gross profit ÷ Capital) × 100

questions.explanation: Net profit margin = (Net profit ÷ Sales) × 100. It shows what percentage of each naira of sales is converted to net profit after all expenses.

questions.question: A cash flow statement shows

options.A: The profit earned by a business during a period
options.B: The inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents during a period
options.C: The financial position of a business at year end
options.D: Only the investing activities of the business

questions.explanation: A cash flow statement (IAS 7) shows how cash was generated and used during a period, classified into operating, investing, and financing activities.

questions.question: Which of the following is classified as a financing activity in a cash flow statement?

options.A: Cash paid to purchase machinery
options.B: Cash received from customers
options.C: Issue of new shares or debentures for cash
options.D: Interest received on investments

questions.explanation: Financing activities relate to changes in the capital structure — issuing shares, repaying loans, paying dividends. Operating activities involve trading; investing activities involve buying/selling long-term assets.

questions.question: Depreciation is added back to net profit in the operating activities section of a cash flow statement because

options.A: It is an income item for the business
options.B: It is a non-cash expense that reduced net profit but did not involve any cash outflow
options.C: It represents cash set aside for replacing assets
options.D: It is a financing activity, not an operating one

questions.explanation: Depreciation is a non-cash expense deducted in arriving at net profit. Since no cash was paid, it is added back to net profit when calculating cash flow from operating activities.

questions.question: In a consignment arrangement, the consignor is

options.A: The person who receives and sells goods on behalf of another
options.B: The person who sends goods to an agent to be sold on their behalf
options.C: The customer who buys goods from the consignee
options.D: The bank that finances the shipment of goods

questions.explanation: The consignor is the owner of goods who sends them to a consignee (agent) to be sold on their behalf. The consignor retains ownership until the goods are sold.

questions.question: Del credere commission is paid to a consignee who

options.A: Sells goods above the agreed minimum price
options.B: Guarantees to pay the consignor even if the buyer defaults on payment
options.C: Returns unsold goods before the agreed period
options.D: Finds new customers for the consignor

questions.explanation: Del credere commission is additional commission paid to the consignee for guaranteeing the payment of debts — if the buyer defaults, the consignee bears the loss, not the consignor.

questions.question: In hire purchase accounting, the asset is recorded in the buyer's books at

options.A: The cash price of the asset
options.B: The total hire purchase price including all interest
options.C: The deposit paid only
options.D: The second-hand value of the asset

questions.explanation: The asset is recorded at its cash (cost) price in the buyer's books. The difference between the total hire purchase price and the cash price is the interest (hire purchase charges) which is allocated over the period.

questions.question: The total hire purchase price minus the cash price equals

options.A: The deposit paid
options.B: The total interest (HP charges) payable over the hire purchase period
options.C: The insurance premium on the asset
options.D: The residual value of the asset

questions.explanation: HP charges (interest) = Total HP price – Cash price. This interest is spread over the HP period as a finance charge in the profit and loss account.

questions.question: In branch accounting, when goods are sent from head office to a branch at cost price plus loading, the loading represents

options.A: The profit already recognised by head office on the goods
options.B: An unrealised profit element in closing stock at branch, which must be eliminated
options.C: The carriage cost of sending goods to the branch
options.D: Insurance charges on goods in transit to the branch

questions.explanation: Loading is the mark-up added to the cost price of goods transferred from head office to branch. It represents an unrealised (notional) profit that must be eliminated in the consolidated accounts when the branch still holds unsold stock.

questions.question: Departmental accounts are prepared to

options.A: Show the overall profit of the entire business
options.B: Show the profit or loss of each department separately to assess performance
options.C: Calculate the tax liability of each department
options.D: Show the wages paid to staff in each department

questions.explanation: Departmental accounts help management assess the performance of each department individually. Common expenses (rent, salaries) are apportioned to each department on a reasonable basis.

questions.question: IFRS stands for

options.A: International Financial Regulation Standards
options.B: International Financial Reporting Standards
options.C: Internal Financial Recording System
options.D: International Federation of Revenue Standards

questions.explanation: IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards) are global accounting standards issued by the International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) to ensure consistency and transparency in financial reporting.

questions.question: The Financial Reporting Council of Nigeria (FRCN) is responsible for

options.A: Collecting corporate taxes on behalf of the government
options.B: Setting and enforcing accounting and auditing standards in Nigeria
options.C: Regulating the activities of commercial banks
options.D: Managing the Nigerian Stock Exchange

questions.explanation: The FRCN (established 2011) promotes and enforces accounting, auditing, and financial reporting standards in Nigeria, replacing the Nigerian Accounting Standards Board (NASB).

questions.question: A nominal (income statement) account records

options.A: Assets and liabilities of the business
options.B: Incomes and expenses of the business
options.C: Personal accounts of debtors and creditors
options.D: The owner's capital account

questions.explanation: Nominal accounts record incomes (sales, rent received, interest received) and expenses (wages, rent paid, depreciation). They are closed off to the profit and loss account at the end of the period.

questions.question: A real account records

options.A: Transactions with named persons (debtors and creditors)
options.B: Assets of the business — both tangible and intangible
options.C: Revenues and expenses of the business
options.D: The capital and drawings of the owner

questions.explanation: Real accounts record assets of the business — tangible assets (buildings, machinery, stock) and intangible assets (goodwill, patents). They have a continuing balance carried forward.

questions.question: At year end, the balance on the purchases account is transferred to

options.A: The balance sheet
options.B: The trading account
options.C: The profit and loss account
options.D: The capital account

questions.explanation: The purchases account balance is transferred to the trading (cost of goods sold) account to calculate gross profit.

questions.question: At year end, the balance on the rent expense account is transferred to

options.A: The trading account
options.B: The balance sheet
options.C: The profit and loss account
options.D: The debtors account

questions.explanation: Rent is an indirect (operating) expense. It is closed off to the profit and loss account at year end, where it is deducted from gross profit to arrive at net profit.

questions.question: The purchase of a motor vehicle on credit is recorded as

options.A: Debit motor vehicle account; Credit cash account
options.B: Debit motor vehicle account; Credit creditor's (supplier's) account
options.C: Debit creditor's account; Credit motor vehicle account
options.D: Debit purchases account; Credit creditor's account

questions.explanation: Buying a fixed asset on credit: the asset increases (debit motor vehicle account) and a liability is created (credit creditor's account). Note: assets purchased on credit go to creditors, not purchases account.

questions.question: When the owner withdraws cash for personal use (drawings), the double entry is

options.A: Debit cash account; Credit drawings account
options.B: Debit drawings account; Credit cash account
options.C: Debit capital account; Credit cash account
options.D: Debit expenses account; Credit capital account

questions.explanation: Drawings reduce the owner's equity. Debit drawings account (reducing capital) and credit cash account (cash leaves the business).

questions.question: Discount allowed is a discount given to

options.A: Suppliers for early payment
options.B: Customers to encourage prompt payment — it is an expense for the business
options.C: Employees as a staff benefit
options.D: Government agencies as a contractual obligation

questions.explanation: Discount allowed is given by the business to its customers (debtors) to encourage prompt payment. It is an expense (debit discount allowed; credit debtor's account).

questions.question: Discount received is a discount given to the business by

options.A: Customers for buying in bulk
options.B: Suppliers for prompt payment — it is income for the business
options.C: The government as a tax relief
options.D: The bank on interest charges

questions.explanation: Discount received is given by suppliers (creditors) to the business for early payment. It is income for the business (debit creditor's account; credit discount received account).

questions.question: In a three-column cash book, the discount column records

options.A: Cash discounts given and received by the business
options.B: Trade discounts given to customers
options.C: Interest charged on overdue accounts
options.D: The difference between list price and invoice price

questions.explanation: A three-column cash book has columns for discount, cash, and bank. The discount columns (debit for discount allowed; credit for discount received) are memorandum columns — they are not part of the double entry but are totalled and posted to the respective discount accounts.

questions.question: A contra entry in the cash book occurs when

options.A: Cash is paid to a creditor by cheque
options.B: Money is transferred between the cash account and the bank account within the business
options.C: A dishonoured cheque is received from a debtor
options.D: Interest is received from the bank

questions.explanation: A contra entry is when cash is deposited into the bank (credit cash, debit bank — marked 'C') or cash is withdrawn from the bank (debit cash, credit bank). Both sides are in the same cash book.

questions.question: An error of original entry occurs when

options.A: A transaction is entered in the wrong ledger account
options.B: The wrong amount is entered in both the debit and credit sides of a transaction
options.C: A transaction is completely omitted from the books
options.D: A debit entry is made instead of a credit entry

questions.explanation: An error of original entry is when the wrong amount is used for both the debit and credit entries. The trial balance still agrees because both sides are wrong by the same amount.

questions.question: The journal entry to correct an error where purchases were understated by ₦5,000 is

options.A: Debit purchases ₦5,000; Credit suspense ₦5,000
options.B: Debit suspense ₦5,000; Credit purchases ₦5,000
options.C: Debit purchases ₦10,000; Credit suspense ₦10,000
options.D: Debit suspense ₦2,500; Credit purchases ₦2,500

questions.explanation: If purchases were understated (recorded as less than actual), purchases must be increased (debited) and the suspense account is credited to clear the difference.

questions.question: Stock should be valued at

options.A: Selling price at year end
options.B: The lower of cost and net realisable value (NRV)
options.C: The higher of cost and net realisable value
options.D: The replacement cost of the stock

questions.explanation: IAS 2 requires stock (inventory) to be valued at the lower of cost and net realisable value (NRV). NRV is the estimated selling price less costs to complete and sell.

questions.question: The FIFO (First In First Out) method of stock valuation assumes that

options.A: The most recently purchased goods are sold first
options.B: The oldest (earliest purchased) goods are sold first
options.C: All goods in stock are identical and any unit can be treated as sold first
options.D: Stock is valued at the most recent purchase price

questions.explanation: FIFO assumes the first goods purchased are the first to be sold. Closing stock is valued at the most recent purchase prices. In a period of rising prices, FIFO gives a higher closing stock value and higher profit.

questions.question: The weighted average cost (AVCO) method of stock valuation

options.A: Uses the cost of the most recently purchased items to value all stock
options.B: Calculates the average cost by dividing the total cost of goods available by the total units available, recomputed after each purchase
options.C: Assigns the cost of the oldest items to goods sold
options.D: Values stock at current replacement cost

questions.explanation: AVCO calculates a new weighted average cost per unit each time a new batch is purchased. All issues of stock are at this average cost.

questions.question: A high stock turnover ratio generally indicates

options.A: The business is holding too much stock
options.B: The business is selling its stock quickly and efficiently
options.C: The business has slow-moving or obsolete stock
options.D: The business is making high profits

questions.explanation: A high stock turnover ratio means stock is being sold and replaced rapidly — indicating efficient management of inventory and strong sales performance.

questions.question: Creditors payment period is calculated as

options.A: (Creditors ÷ Sales) × 365
options.B: (Creditors ÷ Credit purchases) × 365
options.C: (Debtors ÷ Sales) × 365
options.D: (Stock ÷ Purchases) × 365

questions.explanation: Creditors payment period = (Trade creditors ÷ Credit purchases) × 365 days. It shows the average number of days the business takes to pay its suppliers.

questions.question: The gearing ratio measures

options.A: How efficiently a business uses its working capital
options.B: The proportion of a business financed by debt compared to equity
options.C: The speed at which debtors pay the business
options.D: The profitability of the business relative to its sales

questions.explanation: Gearing = Long-term debt ÷ Capital employed (or equity). A highly geared company has a large proportion of debt financing, which increases financial risk.

questions.question: If insurance paid is ₦18,000 for 12 months but the financial year ends after 9 months of the policy, the prepayment is
questions.explanation: Amount relating to current period = 9/12 × ₦18,000 = ₦13,500. Prepayment (relating to next period) = 3/12 × ₦18,000 = ₦4,500.

questions.question: Accrued income (income receivable) on the balance sheet represents

options.A: Income that has been received in advance
options.B: Income earned in the current period but not yet received in cash
options.C: Expenses incurred but not yet paid
options.D: Cash held in the business at year end

questions.explanation: Accrued income (income receivable) is income earned but not yet received — it is a current asset on the balance sheet. Example: interest earned but not yet received from the bank.

questions.question: Which of the following items is shown on the credit side of a trading account?

options.A: Purchases
options.B: Opening stock
options.C: Sales revenue
options.D: Carriage inwards

questions.explanation: The credit side of the trading account shows sales revenue (and closing stock). The debit side shows opening stock, purchases, returns outwards, and carriage inwards.

questions.question: Returns inwards (sales returns) in the trading account are

options.A: Added to sales
options.B: Deducted from sales to give net sales
options.C: Deducted from purchases
options.D: Added to opening stock

questions.explanation: Returns inwards are goods returned by customers. They are deducted from gross sales to give net sales (Net sales = Gross sales – Returns inwards).

questions.question: If gross profit is ₦45,000 and the gross profit margin is 30%, what are the sales?
questions.explanation: Gross profit margin = Gross profit ÷ Sales. So Sales = Gross profit ÷ Gross profit margin = ₦45,000 ÷ 0.30 = ₦150,000.

questions.question: If the mark-up is 25% and cost of goods sold is ₦80,000, what is the gross profit?
questions.explanation: Mark-up = Gross profit ÷ Cost of goods sold. Gross profit = 25% × ₦80,000 = ₦20,000.

questions.question: A machine purchased for ₦100,000 is depreciated at 20% reducing balance. Net book value at end of year 2 is
questions.explanation: Year 1 depreciation = 20% × ₦100,000 = ₦20,000. NBV after year 1 = ₦80,000. Year 2 depreciation = 20% × ₦80,000 = ₦16,000. NBV after year 2 = ₦80,000 – ₦16,000 = ₦64,000.

questions.question: The provision for depreciation account has a

options.A: Debit balance
options.B: Credit balance
options.C: Nil balance at year end
options.D: Balance transferred to the profit and loss account annually

questions.explanation: The provision for depreciation (accumulated depreciation) account has a credit balance. It accumulates year after year and is deducted from the asset's cost on the balance sheet to show the net book value.

questions.question: In a partnership, interest on drawings is

options.A: Credited to the appropriation account and debited to partners' current accounts
options.B: An extra source of capital for the partnership
options.C: Charged to the profit and loss account as an expense
options.D: An amount paid by the partnership to its bankers

questions.explanation: Interest on drawings is a charge against partners to discourage excessive withdrawals. It is a credit to the appropriation account (income for the partnership) and a debit to each partner's current account.

questions.question: A partner's loan to the partnership is entitled to interest at what rate per annum in the absence of a partnership agreement?

options.A: No interest is payable
options.B: 5% per annum
options.C: 10% per annum
options.D: The bank's current lending rate

questions.explanation: Under the Partnership Act (UK/Nigeria), in the absence of a deed, a partner who lends money to the firm beyond their capital is entitled to 5% interest per annum on the loan. This is an expense in the P&L account.

questions.question: Dissolution of a partnership means

options.A: The admission of a new partner to the firm
options.B: The legal ending and winding up of the partnership business
options.C: The conversion of the partnership into a limited company
options.D: One partner retiring while the firm continues

questions.explanation: Dissolution is the legal winding up of the partnership — assets are sold, liabilities are paid, and any remaining balance is distributed to partners according to their capital balances.

questions.question: A rights issue is when a company

options.A: Issues shares to the public for the first time
options.B: Offers new shares to existing shareholders at a price below the current market price, in proportion to their holdings
options.C: Issues bonus shares from reserves
options.D: Repurchases its own shares from the market

questions.explanation: A rights issue gives existing shareholders the right to buy new shares at a discounted price in proportion to their current holdings — they can exercise the right, sell it, or let it lapse.

questions.question: Preference shares that are cumulative mean

options.A: They carry more than one vote per share
options.B: Unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before ordinary shareholders receive anything
options.C: They are always redeemable after a fixed period
options.D: Dividends increase each year at a fixed rate

questions.explanation: Cumulative preference shares mean that if the dividend is not paid in a year (due to insufficient profits), the arrears accumulate and must be paid in full before any dividend can be paid to ordinary shareholders.

questions.question: In a company's balance sheet, proposed dividends are shown as

options.A: Non-current liabilities
options.B: A deduction from retained earnings in equity
options.C: Current liabilities (as an obligation to shareholders)
options.D: An asset of the company

questions.explanation: A proposed (declared but unpaid) dividend is an obligation of the company to its shareholders and is therefore shown as a current liability in the balance sheet.

questions.question: Total debtors account is used in incomplete records to calculate

options.A: Total purchases for the period
options.B: Total credit sales for the period
options.C: Total cash paid to creditors
options.D: Opening capital of the business

questions.explanation: The total debtors (sales ledger control) account is used to find the missing figure for credit sales: Opening debtors + Credit sales – Cash received from debtors – Discount allowed – Bad debts = Closing debtors.

questions.question: Total creditors account is used in incomplete records to calculate

options.A: Total sales for the period
options.B: Total credit purchases for the period
options.C: Cash received from customers
options.D: Closing capital of the business

questions.explanation: The total creditors (purchases ledger control) account is used to find credit purchases: Opening creditors + Credit purchases – Cash paid to creditors – Discount received = Closing creditors.

questions.question: When a bad debt previously written off is recovered, the accounting entry is

options.A: Debit bad debts recovered; Credit debtor's account
options.B: Debit cash/bank account; Credit bad debts recovered account (income)
options.C: Debit provision for bad debts; Credit cash account
options.D: Debit capital account; Credit bad debts account

questions.explanation: When a previously written-off bad debt is recovered: first reinstate the debtor (Debit debtor; Credit bad debts recovered), then record the cash receipt (Debit cash; Credit debtor). The net effect is Debit cash; Credit bad debts recovered (income).

questions.question: If the provision for bad debts is increased, the effect on profit is

options.A: Profit increases
options.B: Profit decreases — the increase in provision is charged to P&L account
options.C: No effect on profit
options.D: Only the balance sheet is affected

questions.explanation: An increase in the provision for bad debts requires an additional charge to the profit and loss account (debit P&L; credit provision for bad debts), thereby reducing net profit.

questions.question: The cost of production transferred to the trading account is

options.A: The prime cost of goods produced
options.B: The total factory cost of goods completed and transferred to finished goods store
options.C: Only the direct material cost of goods produced
options.D: The selling price less profit margin

questions.explanation: The cost of production (or cost of goods manufactured) includes prime cost plus factory overheads, adjusted for opening and closing WIP. This is the cost charged to the trading account for goods completed.

questions.question: If direct materials used are ₦30,000, direct wages ₦20,000, and factory overheads ₦10,000, the factory cost of production is
questions.explanation: Factory cost = Prime cost + Factory overheads = (₦30,000 + ₦20,000) + ₦10,000 = ₦50,000 + ₦10,000 = ₦60,000.

questions.question: The materiality concept means

options.A: Only physical (tangible) assets are recorded
options.B: Minor items may be treated in the simplest and most convenient way — only significant items need strict accounting treatment
options.C: Only transactions above a fixed monetary amount are recorded
options.D: All accounting information must be presented in writing

questions.explanation: Materiality means that only significant items (material in amount or nature) need strict accounting treatment. Immaterial items can be dealt with simply — e.g., cheap items expensed rather than capitalised.

questions.question: The realisation concept states that

options.A: Profits are recognised when cash is received
options.B: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (i.e., when the goods/services are delivered and revenue is earned)
options.C: Expenses are recognised when they are paid
options.D: Assets must be revalued every year to reflect their true value

questions.explanation: The realisation concept states that revenue (profit) is recognised when it is earned — when goods are delivered or services performed — not necessarily when cash is received.

questions.question: Gross pay is

options.A: The amount an employee receives after all deductions
options.B: The total earnings of an employee before any deductions
options.C: The employer's contribution to pension only
options.D: The basic salary excluding overtime and bonuses

questions.explanation: Gross pay is total pay before any deductions (PAYE income tax, pension contributions, NHF, etc.). Net pay (take-home pay) = Gross pay – Deductions.

questions.question: PAYE in payroll accounting stands for

options.A: Pay As You Earn — income tax deducted by the employer from employees' wages
options.B: Pay All Your Expenses
options.C: Pension and Year-End contribution
options.D: Payment Against Year-End assessment

questions.explanation: PAYE (Pay As You Earn) is the system through which income tax (and sometimes other levies) are deducted at source from employees' wages by the employer and remitted to the tax authority.

questions.question: In accounting for VAT, output VAT is

options.A: VAT paid by the business on its purchases
options.B: VAT collected by the business on its sales — a liability owed to the government
options.C: VAT reclaimed by the business from the government
options.D: The net VAT position of the business

questions.explanation: Output VAT is the VAT a business charges its customers on sales. It is collected on behalf of the government and is a liability. Input VAT is paid on purchases. VAT payable = Output VAT – Input VAT.

questions.question: Input VAT is

options.A: VAT charged by the business on goods it sells
options.B: VAT paid by the business on its purchases — which it can reclaim from the government
options.C: VAT charged on imported goods only
options.D: VAT on capital goods only

questions.explanation: Input VAT is the VAT paid by a VAT-registered business on its purchases. It is recoverable (offset) against output VAT, so the business only remits the difference to the tax authority.

questions.question: The purchase of equipment for office use on credit should be recorded in

options.A: The purchases day book
options.B: The general journal (journal proper)
options.C: The cash book
options.D: The sales day book

questions.explanation: The purchase of a fixed asset (equipment) on credit is not a credit purchase of goods for resale, so it does not go in the purchases day book. It is recorded in the general journal with a debit to equipment and a credit to the supplier.

questions.question: Writing off a bad debt in the books requires an entry in

options.A: The sales day book
options.B: The returns inwards day book
options.C: The general journal
options.D: The cash book

questions.explanation: Writing off a bad debt is a non-routine transaction that does not appear in the other books of original entry. It is recorded in the general journal (Debit bad debts; Credit debtor's account).

questions.question: The totals of the sales day book are posted to the ledger as

options.A: Debit to individual debtors' accounts; Credit to sales account
options.B: Debit to sales account; Credit to debtors control account
options.C: Debit to debtors control account; Credit to sales account
options.D: Debit to cash account; Credit to sales account

questions.explanation: From the sales day book: individual transactions are posted to personal debtors' accounts (debit). The total is posted as debit to the sales ledger control account and credit to the sales account.

questions.question: The totals of the purchases day book are posted to the ledger as

options.A: Debit to purchases account; Credit to creditors control account
options.B: Debit to creditors control account; Credit to purchases account
options.C: Debit to cash account; Credit to purchases account
options.D: Debit to purchases account; Credit to cash account

questions.explanation: From the purchases day book: purchases account is debited (expense increases) and the purchases ledger control account (creditors) is credited (liability increases).

questions.question: The historical cost concept requires that

options.A: Assets are recorded at their current replacement cost
options.B: Assets are recorded at their original purchase (acquisition) cost
options.C: Assets are updated to fair value at each balance sheet date
options.D: Only fixed assets are recorded at historical cost

questions.explanation: Under the historical cost concept, assets are recorded at the price paid to acquire them (original cost), not at current market value. This provides objective, verifiable figures.

questions.question: The objectivity concept in accounting requires that

options.A: Accountants must be members of a professional body
options.B: Accounting information must be based on verifiable evidence and free from bias
options.C: All financial statements must be audited annually
options.D: Accountants must use government-approved software

questions.explanation: The objectivity concept means financial information should be based on objective, verifiable evidence (like invoices and receipts) rather than personal opinions, ensuring reliability.

questions.question: If the trial balance shows total debits of ₦500,000 and total credits of ₦498,000, the suspense account is

options.A: Credited with ₦2,000
options.B: Debited with ₦2,000
options.C: Credited with ₦1,000
options.D: Debited with ₦998,000

questions.explanation: When total debits (₦500,000) exceed total credits (₦498,000) by ₦2,000, the suspense account is debited with ₦2,000 to make the trial balance agree. This indicates credits are understated or debits are overstated.

questions.question: A revaluation account in a partnership is used when

options.A: The partnership earns unusually high profits in a year
options.B: Assets and liabilities are revalued on a change in the profit-sharing structure — to ensure fair treatment of partners
options.C: A partner borrows money from the firm
options.D: The firm converts to a limited company

questions.explanation: A revaluation account is used when assets and liabilities are revalued upon a change in the partnership (new partner, retirement, etc.). Profits or losses on revaluation are shared among the partners in the old profit-sharing ratio.

questions.question: A business with current assets of ₦120,000, stock of ₦50,000, and current liabilities of ₦60,000 has an acid test ratio of
questions.explanation: Acid test ratio = (Current assets – Stock) ÷ Current liabilities = (₦120,000 – ₦50,000) ÷ ₦60,000 = ₦70,000 ÷ ₦60,000 = 1.17:1.

questions.question: If net profit is ₦30,000 and capital employed is ₦150,000, the ROCE is
questions.explanation: ROCE = (Net profit ÷ Capital employed) × 100 = (₦30,000 ÷ ₦150,000) × 100 = 20%.

questions.question: A dishonoured cheque in the cash book means

options.A: The cheque is valid but has not been presented yet
options.B: The bank has returned the cheque unpaid — usually due to insufficient funds
options.C: The cheque is post-dated and cannot be cashed yet
options.D: The cheque has been cancelled by the drawer

questions.explanation: A dishonoured (bounced) cheque is returned unpaid by the bank. The cash book debit (receipt) must be reversed — the debtor's account is debited again, restoring the original debt.

questions.question: A debit balance on the purchases ledger control account indicates

options.A: The business owes money to its suppliers
options.B: The business has overpaid a supplier or received a refund — a debtor balance
options.C: Purchases exceed the cash payments made
options.D: There is an error in the purchases day book
